Ah, so it does.  Still, the code is a little hard to follow since reasoning
about its correctness requires pulling together these different little
details from various parts of the code.  It's better if the bodies of
functions are self evidently correct just own their own.  This isn't always
possible, but adding a little more to the if statements in some of these
functions can make them obviously correct independent of the specific error
handling happening in other functions.  Also, it is likely that other
people will edit these functions at some point and they will probably not
consider their effects on code that calls them.  For example, if the error
handling behavior of get_numpy_ndarray_shape() were to change then the
error handling behavior of the function we are talking about could easily
become incorrect.

If we speak about `comment block of code` vs `extract it as a named function`, named functions are preferable imo. Comments tend to become obsolete much faster then function names, and you usually have more motivation for fixing names than for fixing comments (well, at least for me :) ). And interesting way to bind a comment to a piece of code is to write a descriptive commit message (http://mislav.net/2014/02/hidden-documentation/).

But extracting a functions which will be used only once has other advantages. 

Consider a hundred lines long function. To get an idea of how it works, you need to skim through all this lines. However if the function was decomposed into ten more or less isolated and properly named sub functions, you'll have to read only ten lines of code. To make this more concrete, I find [this](https://github.com/netvl/intellij-rust/blob/4206e9102ca510df023ca830945d1601e3e50d79/src/main/kotlin/org/rust/ide/intentions/AddDerive.kt#L20-L27) a lot more readable than [this](https://github.com/netvl/intellij-rust/blob/52ea71b6cf2e0a87d000158befba18460907cf73/src/main/kotlin/org/rust/ide/intentions/AddDerive.kt#L20-L41).

Another advantage is that by extracting a function you explicitly isolate necessary context and pass it as parameters, which immediately gives you a sense of how data flows through the program. This is also an instance of "minimize visibility" advice, which is especially important in the languages without static scopes  :)

@YakBizzarro , sorry for the late comment, but I would like to rename this class to ScaledParameter. The reason is the following. A "scaler" (so far as I my knowledge of English is concerned) is smth that "scales" smth. According to the implementation, the internal "multiplier" parameter is doing the actual "scaling", while the "ParameterScaler" instance just returns the "scaled" value of the wrapped parameter. In other words, if I call `get` for the "ParameterScaler" I get the "scaled" value. Thus, "ParameterScaler" shall be called "ScaledParameter".

So, I don't do the API check on update, because the update statements are always standalone.
```Python
if foo:
  instance.foo = foo
if bar:
  instance.bar = bar
```

The problem occurs during creates:
```Python
thing = Thing(foo=foo)
if bar:
  thing.bar = bar
```
If bar depends on an API version, it will break if someone refactors this to:
```Python
thing = Python(foo=foo, bar=bar)
```

This can't happen in the update scenario, so I don't use the API checks in any update scenarios.

The easiest compilation option here probably is:
```
.filter{it, m -> (!it.alias.intersects($wanted).empty)}
```

That means the Gremlin format for `intersects` would be: 
```
u'(!{left}.{operator}({right}).empty)'
```

OK, I think I might see your point here.  You are pointing out that this is not the correct SB profile of a _star_, since star's have an SED that is not included here.  

I think the point you are making is already obvious with respect to galaxies -- that if you use a regular GSObject, then it corresponds to a flat SED.  But maybe it's not as obvious that stars also have SEDs that are not included here in the PSF object.  So here is a proposed paragraph that maybe makes this point more clearly:

Note that a ChromaticOpticalPSF by itself is NOT the correct thing to use to draw an image of a
star.  Stars (and galaxies too, of course) have an SED that is not flat.  To draw a real star, you
should either multiply the ChromaticOpticalPSF object by an SED, or convolve it by a point
source (typically approximated by a very tiny Gaussian) multiplied by an SED:

    >>> psf = galsim.ChromaticOpticalPSF(...)
    >>> star = galsim.Gaussian(sigma = 1.e-6) * psf_sed
    >>> final_star = galsim.Convolve( [psf, star] )
    >>> final_star.drawImage(bandpass = bp, ...)

I've expanded the doc string some what.  But I'll explain in more detail
here and based on feedback, I'll remodify the docstring.

The call can reorder and delete tracks.  In general the underlying call
expects two string arguments, tracks which indicate the 'current' track
position you want to modify and the new_pos that dictate what should happen
to it.  You can, in a single request, specify moving and deleting, but they
happen grouped into two calls.  First doing all the delete requests in an
op, and then doing the moves (however that is with respect to the new track
positions after the deletes.  If you delete a track, all following tracks
are immediately renumbered to fill in the gap. So any move requests need to
take this into account.  It is simpler to think about if you make the
changes individually.

So the first option of list of ints.   tracks=[1,3], new_pos=[0, None]
(since it is using ints, I chose to indicate a delete with None) The
results of this operation would move the second track into the first
position, what was in the 1 position moves to the second postion and the
4th track is deleted from the playlist.

trackA       pos:0
trackB       pos:1
trackC       pos:2
trackD       pos:3

is transformed into:
trackB      pos:0
trackA      pos:1
trackC      pos:2

When the inputs are lists, each pair zip(tracks, new_pos) creates an
underlying call to avTransport.ReorderTracksInSavedQueue.

The same call using a list of int-likes  would look like:  tracks=['1',
'3'], new_pos['0', ''], which would also create two underlying calls.
(note: when using int-likes a delete is specified by an empty string.)

Now, if you really wanted to have fun, you can call reorder with strings,
tracks='1,3,5', new_pos='0,2,4' which would move the 2nd, 4th and 6th track
into the first, third and 5th position in a single underlying call. Using a
string, says: "I know what I'm doing, get out of my way."  It seems to
happen "atomically"  in that the moves happen before the track order
shifts.  However, getting this right can be frustrating when you have
deletes going on at the same time.   Using the helper functions clear, move
and remove make it simpler to think about because the list is being
transformed one step at a time and you only need to supply simple parameters

This method of having two mirrored imports that set one to the None type is something I found confusing. After digging through the Django contenttypes source, I see that what is happening is trying to deal with a rename of the descriptor from 1.8 to 1.9. Unfortunately, this method leads to extra branches in an already large if/elif section.

How about using an import alias instead?

```python
if django.VERSION >= (1, 9):
    # ... snip unmodified lines ...
    from django.contrib.contenttypes.fields import ReverseGenericManyToOneDescriptor
else:
    from django.contrib.contenttypes.fields import ReverseGenericRelatedObjectsDescriptor as ReverseGenericManyToOneDescriptor
```
I believe this would clean up the code, be more efficient, and capture the fact that the descriptor was renamed. It seems this pattern was already followed for the first two imports that follow the `else`.

Yes it does it indirectly. The documentation you are referring to below also says the same. The delimiter in the example is `/` so whenever a user specifies a delimiter, it gives the matching objects in `prefixes` key in the response json: >In conjunction with the prefix filter, the use of the delimiter parameter allows the list method to operate like a directory listing, despite the object namespace being flat. For example, if delimiter were set to "/", then listing objects from a bucket that contains the objects "a/b", "a/c", "d", "e", "e/f" would return objects "d" and "e", and prefixes "a/" and "e/" As you see below I replicate the example: When you just pass `/` as delimiter, the response api will produce the following result: Clearly, there are just 2 folders (i.e 2 objects that end with `/`) which are returned in the `prefixes` field. Now, if you change the example to have following objects in the bucket (i.e. just renaming `d` to `d.py`) as below: and if you now request the api with `.py` delimiter, it would result in just `d.py` in the `prefixes` key: So, the idea is whenever you have pass a value to `delimiter`, you should take the value from `prefixes` in the api response. If you do not pass anything to `delimiter`, you would not have `prefixes` in the api response, so you take the value of `name` field of the api response as shown below: The above image shows that when you just pass `prefix` to the api, the response json would have no `prefixes` key and instead store all the matching objects with that prefix in the `items` list and all individual object names (including the full path to that object) is stored in `items.name`.
